title: GPU Instances
subtitle: NVIDIA GPUs on demand — no waitlists

steps.label: The Analogy
steps.body: A GPU Linode is like renting a Formula 1 car instead of a sedan. You don't need it for grocery runs (web servers), but for the race track (ML training, inference), nothing else comes close.

steps.label: How It Works
steps.body:
Linode GPU instances expose dedicated NVIDIA GPUs with pre-installed drivers:

- RTX 4000 Ada Generation (16GB VRAM)
- Up to 4 GPUs per instance
- NVIDIA CUDA toolkit pre-configured
- Full root access — install any framework (PyTorch, TensorFlow, vLLM, TGI)
- Available in select regions (verify with `linode-cli linodes types | grep gpu`)

steps.label: Real World
steps.body: Provision a single-GPU instance: `linode-cli linodes create --type g1-gpu-rtx4000-1 --region us-ord --image linode/ubuntu24.04`. Install Docker + NVIDIA Container Toolkit, then run any GPU workload in containers. For multi-GPU training, provision multiple GPU Linodes and use distributed frameworks like DeepSpeed or FSDP.
"GPU Linodes = dedicated NVIDIA GPUs with full root access. No reserved instances, no spot interruptions, flat hourly pricing."

quiz.question: What GPU hardware is available on Linode GPU instances?

options.explain: Linode uses NVIDIA GPUs, not AMD.
Linode offers RTX 4000 Ada GPUs with 16GB VRAM per card.
TPUs are Google-proprietary. Linode uses NVIDIA GPUs.
Linode uses NVIDIA GPUs for ML workloads.

quiz.question: How do you check GPU availability in a specific region?

options.explain: You can check programmatically via CLI.
This lists all GPU plan types and their availability across regions.
GPU instances are in select regions only — always verify.
GPU instances are managed through Cloud Manager or linode-cli, not the CDN dashboard.
